For a web project like yours, the best "Industry Standard" is Matter.js. It’s lightweight, 2D, and handles collisions and forces beautifully. 2.content:
The "Physics Engine" Plan:
The World: You create a hidden "Physics World" that runs in the background.

The Bodies: Every star in Konva is linked to a "Body" in Matter.js.

The Loop: Every frame (60fps), Matter.js calculates where the stars should be, and Konva simply draws them at those coordinates.

4.content:
Why this is "100x" Magical:
Tactile Feeling: When you "throw" a star across the screen, it will glide and slowly slow down due to air friction.

Orbiting: If you balance the forces right, you can actually make small stars orbit larger ones, creating a literal solar system of your thoughts.

Collision Energy: You can make stars "glow brighter" for a second when they collide, adding a visual reward for interaction.

Without Barnes-Hut, the browser tries to calculate $1,000 \times 1,000$ connections every frame (1 million calculations). With it, we reduce that to about $1,000 \times \log(1,000)$, making it buttery smooth even on a phone. [Pasted text #3] [Pasted text #4 +5 lines]

1.content:
1. The "Initial Kick" (Orbital Velocity)
In a real universe, if stars only had gravity, they would all just slam into each other and become one giant ball. They stay apart because they have Angular Momentum.

The Logic: When a new post is "born," don't just place it. Give it a random tangential velocity vector.

The Result: Instead of falling straight into the center of a galaxy, the star will enter a stable Orbit. Your galaxies will actually spin like the Milky Way because of the balance between gravity pulling in and velocity pushing out.

4.content:
Why this works for the "Long Game":
True Simulation: If you leave the app open, you will see your "Writing Universe" actually evolve. Galaxies will merge, stars will be ejected into deep space, and clusters will form.

Scientific "Weight": When users ask how it works, you aren't just saying "I put them there." You’re saying: "The universe self-organizes based on the semantic gravity of your thoughts." That is a killer marketing hook.

Infinite Scalability: The Quadtree is how professional astronomers simulate billions of stars. By starting here, you never have to rewrite your engine again.

Task: Stabilize Orbital Physics and Fix Star Clumping

1. Slow Down Rotation:

In the orbital velocity calculation, apply a speedFactor of 0.2.

Ensure the velocity vector is calculated using the square root of the distance to the center (Newtonian Orbit), but scaled down for a "peaceful" visual.

2. Implement Anti-Clumping (Pauli Exclusion):

Add a Separation Force. If two stars are closer than 50px, apply an immediate outward force to push them apart. This prevents them from "sticking" together.

3. Fix 'User Name' Centering:

The User's Name (The Sun) should be set to isStatic: true in Matter.js so it doesn't get pushed around by the posts.

Increase the Mass of the Sun star so the posts have a clear center to orbit.

4. Angular Damping:

Increase frictionAir to 0.07 and set inertia: Infinity on the star bodies. This prevents the stars themselves from spinning like crazy while they orbit.

1.content: This is the "Event Horizon" mechanic. To make it feel immersive, you don't want a sudden jump to a new page; you want a visual "docking" sequence where the star expands until it swallows the screen, leading seamlessly into the ContentViewer.Here is how to prompt Claude to build the Proximity Detection and the Transition Logic.1. The Logic: "The Event Horizon"We need to define a "Docking Distance." In your pseudo-3D math, as $z$ approaches $0$, the star gets larger.The Threshold: When $z$ reaches a very small value (e.g., $0.1$), the user is "inside" the star.The Action: Stop the simulation, play a "Warp" effect, and trigger the ContentViewer.2. The HUD: "Targeting System"To make it mobile-friendly and intuitive, the user needs to know they are "locked on" to a post.The UI: When a star is near the center of the screen, draw a "Targeting Bracket" around it.The Feedback: As you get closer, the bracket should turn from Cyan to Red, and the star should begin to glow brighter.

1.content:
Add a phase system to the apartment blueprint editor so building elements can be assigned to construction/design phases.

Requirements:
- create a Phase domain model with id, name, order, color, and visibility settings
- allow objects such as columns, beams, slabs, walls, stairs, rooms, doors, and windows to store a phaseId
- add UI for creating, renaming, deleting, reordering, and selecting phases
- support 3 viewing modes:
  1. all phases
  2. single selected phase only
  3. cumulative mode showing all objects up to the selected phase order
- apply phase filtering consistently in:
  - plan view
  - section/elevation rendering where applicable
  - sheet export
  - Three.js 3D preview
- keep phase logic separate from domain geometry logic
- do not hardcode object types to specific phases; let phases be user-assignable
- keep the architecture modular and future-ready for phase-specific sheets, phase colors, and simple phase animation in 3D
- do not break the current multi-floor and export systems

1.content:
Refactor the phase system so it supports both default templates and full user customization.

Requirements:
- phases must be fully data-driven
- each phase should have id, name, order, color, and visibility settings
- objects should reference phases by phaseId, never by phase name
- when creating a new project, allow choosing:
  1. default construction phase template
  2. empty custom phase setup
- users must be able to:
  - add phases
  - rename phases
  - reorder phases
  - recolor phases
  - delete phases
- preserve existing phase filtering modes:
  - all phases
  - single phase
  - cumulative phases
- do not hardcode object types to fixed phase names
- keep the phase system modular and future-ready for multiple templates and phase-specific sheet exports
- do not break current floor, sheet, export, or Three.js workflows

Harden the project persistence system for the apartment blueprint web app.

- separate project data from temporary UI/workspace state

- keep autosave in localStorage for recovery
- add validation on load for malformed or incomplete project files
- add a migration pipeline so older schema versions can be upgraded later
- preserve stable object IDs and reference-based relationships
- do not break current floors, roof, truss, sheet, export, and Three.js systems
- keep the persistence layer modular and future-ready for IndexedDB, Node.js backend, and Supabase cloud storage

1.content:
Build Sketch V1 into a usable object-design workspace.

Requirements:
- keep Sketch separate from Floorplan domain logic
- use the shared geometry, SVG rendering, sheet/export, and Three.js foundations where useful
- create a Sketch project model for designing standalone objects
- support a first constrained object system based on simple parts:
  - Panel
  - Leg
  - Frame
  - Cutout
  - Hole
  - Dimension
- allow users to assemble simple objects like tables, desks, shelves, cabinets, and bed frames
- support orthographic drafting views:
  - top
  - front
  - side
- support a simple read-only 3D preview
- support object grouping/assembly
- support a properties panel for part dimensions and placement
- support saving/loading Sketch projects as JSON
- support sheet/export output for Sketch objects
- do not overbuild freeform modeling yet
- focus on a clean, modular architecture that proves the same core engine can support both floorplan and object design
